title: How to Use Illustrate Ai from DesignBundles: 2024 Step by Step Guide!
description:
How to Use Illustrate Ai from DesignBundles: 2024 Step by Step Guide!

Welcome to this in-depth tutorial on mastering Illustrate AI, the innovative image generation tool from DesignBundles, updated for 2024! In this video, we'll explore the revamped interface, new features, and provide step-by-step guidance to harness the power of AI for creating stunning visuals. Whether you're a beginner or an experienced user, this tutorial is tailored to enhance your skills in image generation.

Chapters:
00:00 Introduction to Illustrate AI 2024 Update
00:14 Interface Elements and How to Prompt
06:52 Image Ratio and Sizes
08:13 Art Styles Explained
11:13 Your Portfolio
12:15 AI Library
12:48 Remix
13:19 Prompt Engineering

🔔 Stay Updated:

Subscribe to our channel for more tutorials
Hit the bell icon to get notified about our latest updates and features
Thank you for watching! If you found this tutorial helpful, please give it a thumbs up, share it with fellow creators, and subscribe for more conte
transcript: Hello, and welcome to the first tutorial of the year.  Illustrate AI from Design Bundles has a new interface, and more features are being added  this year that will allow you to generate even better images and give you more control.  On the main page, click on Illustrate AI, then you'll see two buttons, one for start  creating, and another to explore the gallery.  Let's create some cool images.  Even if you haven't used Illustrate AI before, you can start right away and learn about  it by looking for the I icon that shows you more information about each element on the  interface.  In this video, we will explore what each element does, but it's also useful in case you forget  something, you can simply mouse over the I for more information.  Let's talk about the text prompt.  Here you enter a descriptive text to guide the AI in generating your image.  Be as specific as possible to achieve the best results.  Include details like subjects, colors, mood, and setting.  Keep in mind that the words at the beginning are more important than the ones at the end,  so always put the subject or what is important first.  Let's give it a quick test with something simple, like a cute cartoon cat, and then we'll  move on to more advanced prompts.  For the negative prompt, you specify what you don't want to appear in your generation.  For instance, you can mention objects, art styles, or colors you wish to avoid.  For example, if you don't want your cat to be white, you can add white or color white  in the negative prompt.  Now it's time to choose the art style you have in mind.  You have a variety of styles to choose from, such as digital art, photographic, 3D, and  more.  If you're having trouble deciding, you can think like this.  If it's a photo, go with photographic.  If it's an illustration, go with digital art.  The image count allows you to choose how many different images you want to generate at  once.  You can select from a minimum of one to a maximum of four images.  For size, I will choose a 1.1 ratio, which is the square format, and then I'll hit create  to start generating the image.  In a few seconds, you will see a message indicating that your image has been generated  successfully.  As you can see, I didn't get any white cat because I had white in the negative prompt.  I received a gray and an orange cat.  Now if I don't want any orange cat generation, I can simply include orange in the negative  prompt.  You will notice that with the same prompt, we got some gray and white cats, but none of  them are orange.  It's important to keep in mind that some words have more weight, so the negative prompt  may not always work perfectly.  Now let's make the prompt more specific.  I want a white and adorable cat with green eyes, possibly with some grass and flowers in  the background.  In the negative prompt, I don't want the cat to be ugly, a photo, a comic, or a 3D render.  I also don't want low quality images, signatures, or words on it.  As you can see, I got some nice results that match the detailed prompt.  If you click on an image, you can view it in a larger size.  You'll also find an arrow that allows you to navigate through all four generated images.  Once you find an image you like, you can click the Publish button to start upscaling the  image and have it published on the Illustrate AI library.  You will notice a Publishing button on the top left of the image, so which will turn purple  and display published when it's ready for download.  It's a good idea to navigate through them, to see them in a larger size, and then publish  the one you like.  Additionally, there is now an option to delete a generation.  As you can see, we have only three images left from the original four generations.  In this new interface, you have the option to choose how the generations are displayed.  For example, you can view them as one column, but depending on your device, you might  prefer one of the other arrangements.  You can also use two columns or four columns, so feel free to play around and see which  display works best for you.  When you hover over the i icon, you can see more information about that generation, including  the date, ratio, and scale.  Next to it, there is the Remix button.  Let me show you how it works.  If you click on it, it will copy the prompt, negative prompt, and all the settings of that  generation.  As you can see, the prompt has changed.  If I click again on the first one, I get those settings back.  If I go to an older generation, you can see that on this one, I have a long negative prompt.  With the one in bold, as the positive prompt, and the one below, as the negative prompt.  So now you can easily load those generation settings with the Remix button.  You can also see that it changed the art style, and the ratio is loaded as well.  If there is nothing in the top right of the image, it's a square ratio, but if you see  a purple button there, it's a different ratio, either two, three, or three point two, and  hopefully more ratios will be available soon.  You can observe that if an image is waiting to be published, it says publishing in green,  and once it's published, it changes to the word published in purple.  If you choose the three point two ratio, it will give you a landscape image as indicated  by the purple icon.  Clicking on the image allows you to view the entire landscape image.  You can publish your favorite to obtain a big upscaled image.  Now let's switch to a 2.3 ratio to create a portrait image.  More features are on the way, and soon you'll be able to upload your own image, and get  an interpretation of that image similar to image to image.  Additionally, doll E3 is in the plans for the future.  This means that Illustrate AI is becoming more powerful, and providing users with greater  control over image generation.  Once an image is published, you can find the purple, published button.  Hovering your mouse over it will reveal three dots that allow you to either download  or delete the image.  You can also click on the image itself, which I find easier as it provides the same download  and delete options.  Once the image is downloaded, you can open it and use it in your projects.  As you can see, it's a high resolution image, suitable for various applications.  If you select the 1.1 ratio and click the Create button, you will receive a square image.  As this image is published, you will obtain an upscaled version in JPG format with the  dimensions I'm showing here.  If you select the 3.2 ratio and click the Create button, you will receive a landscape image.  When you download it, the image will have the dimensions I'm showing here.  For the 2.3 ratio, you will obtain a portrait image with the dimensions I'm showing here  when you download it.  When you're talking about an image with a ratio of 2.3, it means the width of the image  is 2 units, and the height of the image is 3 units.  Imagine you have a rectangular picture frame, and the frame is 2 inches wide and 3 inches tall.  If you put an image in that frame, the image would have a 2-3 ratio, meaning it's 2 units  wide for every 3 units in height.  So in simple terms, the 2-3 ratio for an image describes its width in relation to its height,  the width being 2 parts and the height being 3 parts.  At the time of creating this video, it appears that the ratio may not be exact.  Hopefully, in the future, they will add more formats that can be used for platforms like  YouTube and other social media without the need for cropping.  Default.  The standard setting, offering a balanced mix of realism and artistic elements without  specific style emphasis.  3D model creates images that resemble three-dimensional models, often with realistic textures and lighting.  Analog film mimics the look of traditional film photography, often with grainy textures  and the slightly faded color palette.  Anime reflects the style of Japanese animation characterized by vibrant colors exaggerated  facial expressions and stylized features.  Matic produces images that resemble movie scenes, often with dramatic lighting, high contrast  and a sense of motion or narrative.  Comic book emulates the style of graphic novels and comic books with bold outlines, bright  colors, and paneled layouts.  Digital art offers a clean computer-generated appearance often with smooth lines and a polished  finish.  Fantasy art creates fantastical and imaginative scenes, often featuring mythical creatures  magical elements and otherworldly landscapes.  Isometric generates images with an isometric perspective where objects are displayed in  a three-dimensional form but without perspective for shortening.  Line art focuses on the use of lines without much shading or color, emphasizing outlines  and contours.  Low poly imitates a digital art style with a minimalistic approach, using geometric shapes  and a limited number of polygons.  Modeling compound resembles sculptures or models made from materials like clay, with  a tactile and malleable appearance, neon punk, combines neon colors and futuristic elements,  often with a rebellious or edgy undertone reminiscent of cyberpunk aesthetics or agami.  Imitates the Japanese art of paper folding, creating images that look like they are crafted  from folded paper.  Produces images that resemble real-life photographs, with realistic details and textures.  Pixel art emulates the look of early digital art and video games using a grid of colored  squares or pixels to create images.  Tile texture generates images that resemble tiled surfaces with patterns and textures,  however, currently, it doesn't create seamless patterns yet.  To generate patterns or textures, you need to prompt for them specifically by including  words like patterns or texture in your prompt.  Enhance is similar to the default setting but with a slight enhancement.  I mostly use digital art, photographic or cinematic styles for my image generation,  but the style you choose can depend on your personal preference and the specific look  you want to achieve.  On Illustrate AI, if you look at the top right corner, you will find some buttons, clicking  on the your portfolio button will take you to your page with all the images you've published.  Here it displays your username, in this case, Pixel Roma, along with the total number of  publishes and upvotes you've received.  You can use filters to search or sort your images.  These filters allow you to view only squares or horizontal or vertical images, as well as  sort them by art style.  You can also filter between popular and new images.  On the right side, there is a Share button that allows you to share your portfolio on  Facebook, Twitter or Pinterest.  Additionally, you can click on the View Store button to access your store if you or the  creators have one.  In this case, it opens Pixel Roma's store with all the cut files available.  Finally, there is a button for Illustrate AI in the Navigation Bar, which you can use  to return to the Illustrate AI platform any time.  If you click on the AI Library button, you will be taken to the main library where you  can find all the images published by various users.  Here you can search, remix and download any image from the library provided you have a  plus unlimited subscription.  Similar to your portfolio library here in the AI library, you can search for what interests  you or apply filters to narrow down your search and find specific images.  If you come across an interesting image like this bouquet of flowers, you can vote for it,  click on it, and then either download it as it is or remix it to get a variation of the  image.  Notice how it takes all the settings from the original generation such as the prompt  and art style.  Since you don't get the exact seed, the images you create through remixing will be variations  of the original, not exactly the same.  Here is a cute bunny painting.  Let's see how I created it.  You can check the text prompt I used and the negative prompt.  Sometimes I use a short negative, and sometimes I add many words to specify what I don't want.  I wanted it to look like a cartoon watercolor.  I added words like 3D render, photographic, and a comics to the negative prompt since  I didn't want any of those styles.  I also included words that typically make the image less appealing, like mutated and duplicate.  How much each word affects the image individually depends on the prompt, but as a whole, it usually  does a good job in achieving the desired result.  Now let's take another example.  Here I wanted to create a wallpaper with a heart and use the photographic style to make  it look more realistic.  In the negative prompt, I added words like painting, comics, and other terms that could  make it look less desirable.  Watermark and specific words sometimes help to remove unwanted elements from the images.  Here's another example of a prompt created with chat GPT.  Again, I added 3D render and photographic to the negative prompt since I wanted it to  look like a painting, and it turned out really cute.  In this glass, with a drink example, I wanted a photographic style so I used cinematic  since the lighting is more interesting than in a typical photographic style.  Sometimes I add 3D render to the negative to make it look less artificial.  Using words that make it look less photographic can result in a more realistic image.  If you need more inspiration, you can visit the AI library and search for Pixa Roma.  Click on an image I created.  Go to my profile and you can find my generations to remix and experiment with.  For example, with the cute bunny, if you click on remix.  To get the settings and then hit create, you can generate variations of it.  If you enjoyed this video and want to see more, you can find a button to watch tutorials  on the Illustrate AI library.  It will take you to a playlist with only Illustrate AI tutorials.  Thank you for watching.  Like and subscribe for more cool videos.  Have a great day.
